exercises.title: ICE PROBE
exercises.brief: Is the key inside the sorted vault?
promptLines: `sorted` is ascending. Define **contains(sorted, x)** returning `true`/`false` using **binary search** (halve the range each step).
starterLines: // TODO: lo/hi pointers, halve each step
solutionLines: if (sorted[mid] < x) lo = mid + 1; else hi = mid - 1;
exercises.hint: lo<=hi loop; compare midpoint; move lo or hi.
exercises.lore: Halve the search space until the ICE cracks.

exercises.title: SIGNAL TALLY
exercises.brief: Count how many times the ping repeats.
promptLines: Define **countOccurrences(arr, x)** returning how many times `x` appears in `arr`. A single left-to-right scan is fine.
exercises.hint: Start a counter at 0 and add 1 for every value equal to x.
exercises.lore: Every echo of the signal logged on the tally board.

exercises.title: FIRST TRACE
exercises.brief: Pin the first slot holding the target.
promptLines: Define **firstIndex(arr, x)** returning the index of the **first** element equal to `x`, or **-1** if none. Write the loop yourself — no `indexOf`.
starterLines: // TODO: scan and return the first matching index, else -1
exercises.hint: Loop by index; return i on the first match; return -1 after the loop.
exercises.lore: The scanner stops at the first slot that lights up.

exercises.title: RANK CIPHER
exercises.brief: Pull the k-th smallest from the heap.
promptLines: Define **kthSmallest(arr, k)** returning the **k-th smallest** value (1-based) by sorting a **copy** of the array ascending. `arr` always has at least `k` numbers. Do not mutate the input.
starterLines: // TODO: sort a copy ascending, then take position k
solutionLines: const copy = arr.slice().sort((a, b) => a - b);
exercises.hint: arr.slice().sort((a,b)=>a-b) then index k-1 — slice keeps the original intact.
exercises.lore: Sort the stolen keys; the k-th one unlocks the rank cipher.

exercises.title: SIGNAL CREST
exercises.brief: Find a slot no lower than its neighbors.
promptLines: Define **findPeakIndex(arr)** returning the index of the **first** element that is `>=` both its neighbors (edges count, comparing only the side that exists). `arr` has at least one element, so a peak always exists.
starterLines: // TODO: return first index >= its existing neighbors
solutionLines: const okRight = i === n - 1 || arr[i] >= arr[i + 1];
exercises.hint: Scan left to right; an element is a peak when it's >= every neighbor that exists.
exercises.lore: Climb the signal until no neighbor rises above the crest.
